title: A Touch of Evil: The Supernatural Game - Part Two - A Lot of Stuff Happens
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_FzLqe9KziY

Daniel (@dungeondive) returns with part two — and discovers he set up wrong: every available town elder should be deployed, not a random six. The fix gives him nine elders (one already lost). What follows is a rapid argument for why *A Touch of Evil* repays patience: a single round of three hero turns has, by his count, generated "an entire novel's worth of plot," and he's only documenting the moments that genuinely earned a video.

The damage list: a *Full Moon* mystery card permanently buffs all minions and werewolves by +1 combat. On Maria's first turn the advanced co-op chart triggers a surprise showdown at a random lair card — the Church — where Reverend Harding is immediately revealed as the Grand Inquisitor and defects to the Ghost Captain. The Captain rolls 7d6 (he gains +2 fight dice against any hero with under three cursed gold), KOs Argot, and teleports out. Argot wakes possessed by *Madness* and attacks Henrik. Meanwhile an *Order's Influence* card lands on Dr. Manning, weaponising the doctor's office; back-to-back, an *Autopsy* event sets up six investigation tokens contingent on a dead elder in that same room — randomly drawn, thematically welded.

Daniel also pre-positions militia in the icy waters as a future-tense ambush for the Ghost Ship. The game leans into "super talisman" — low player agency, high narrative agency — and he argues that's the trade he keeps coming back for.

----

When randomness reliably produces a coherent story, is the game writing it, or are you?
